1:
The witnesses open the chapter awed by a single, unrepeatable fact: that God 'would take up Elijah into heaven by a whirlwind.' Matthew Henry, whose exposition dominates the verse, marvels that a man 'subject to like passions as we are' should be so 'dignified, thus distinguished, as a man whom the King of kings did delight to honour,' and offers three reasons for it — God looked back on Elijah's eminent services, looked down on 'the present dark and degenerate state of the church' to give 'a very sensible proof of another life after this,' and looked forward to the gospel, making the translation 'a type and figure of the ascension of Christ and the opening of the kingdom of heaven to all believers.' John Wesley gives the same reading almost word for word, that Elijah was 'translated, body and soul, to heaven, only undergoing such a change as was necessary to qualify him for being an inhabitant in that world of Spirits,' a proof of immortality 'in that dark and degenerate age.'

The grammatical-historical voices add precision where the others add wonder. Charles Ellicott notes the compiler prefixed this sentence 'by way of connection with the general thread of the history,' places the event 'in the beginning of the reign of Jehoram,' and reads the Hebrew closely: 'into heaven' is an accusative of direction, while the Septuagint's cautious 'as into heaven' hints 'that not the visible heavens, but God, was the real goal of the prophet's ascension' — an observation the debate over v.11 will press further. He even sites Gilgal in Ephraim, a ring of standing stones from which 'the prophet could take a last look at the whole country which had been the scene of his earthly activity.'

Beneath these angles the witnesses are united. John Gill reads Elijah's wandering from town to town as movement 'under a divine direction,' for God 'had notice' given him of his end; Adam Clarke observes that the coming translation was revealed 'not only to Elijah himself, but also to Elisha, and to the schools of the prophets,' so that 'they were all expecting this solemn event.' The chapter thus begins where Genesis 5:24 left Enoch — a second man who 'never tasted death' — and where the disciples would one day stand gazing after their ascending Lord in Acts 1:9. What unites the fathers of this exposition is the pastoral point Henry draws out: that those 'who have their conversation in heaven, while here on earth, shall be there shortly.'
2:
Three times Elijah says 'Tarry here, I pray thee,' and three times Elisha refuses; the witnesses concentrate on the motive behind the repeated dismissal. Two readings compete. John Gill lays out the traditional options — Elijah spoke 'either out of modesty, not affecting the spread of the honour and glory to be conferred upon him; or to prevent the grief of Elisha at his departure, or to try whether Elisha knew any thing of it.' Adam Clarke narrows these to two, that Elijah asked it 'through humility, not wishing any person to be witness of the honour conferred on him by God, or with the desire to prove the fidelity of Elisha.' John Wesley multiplies the possibilities further still: that being left alone Elijah 'might better prepare himself for his great change,' or spare Elisha the overwhelming sight, or 'try his love, and whet his desire to accompany him.'

Against all of these Charles Ellicott registers a sharp dissent. The request was made, he insists, 'not to test Elisha's affection, nor from a motive of humility, that Elisha might not witness his glorious ascension, but because Elijah was uncertain whether it was God's will that Elisha should go with him' — and 'Elisha's threefold refusal to leave him settled the doubt.' On this reading the drama is not a staged test but a genuine question resolved by the servant's persistence, an angle Ellicott ties to the threefold questioning of John 21:15.

The commentators agree, however, on the errand and the oath. Elijah goes to Bethel because 'the Lord hath sent me,' which Wesley calls 'truth, tho' not the whole truth,' since the prophet was bound for a far longer journey — first to the schools of the prophets 'that he might comfort, and strengthen their hearts in God's work, and give them his dying counsels.' Ellicott explains that Gilgal and Bethel, as 'ancient Canaanite sanctuaries,' had become 'centres of illegal worship,' and the prophetic guilds may have been planted 'to counteract this evil influence at its head-quarters.' Elisha's reply — 'As the LORD liveth, and as thy soul liveth, I will not leave thee' — Ellicott marks as 'a more solemn and emphatic oath' than either half alone, literally 'By the life of Jehovah and by the life of thy soul.' The witnesses land together on Elisha's constancy: whatever Elijah's motive, the disciple will not be shaken off, and his refusal to tarry becomes the model of a love that clings to its master to the very end.
3:
The sons of the prophets at Bethel come out with a startling question — 'Knowest thou that the LORD will take away thy master from thy head to day?' — and the witnesses ask first how they knew, and then what Elisha's curt reply means. On the source of their knowledge there is unanimity: John Wesley says 'this was revealed to some of the sons of the prophets, and by them to the whole college,' Adam Clarke that 'it was a matter well known to all the sons of the prophets,' and Charles Ellicott that 'the prophetic college had been divinely forewarned of Elijah's departure.' Keil and Delitzsch add the theological reason: 'The Lord had therefore revealed to the disciples of the prophets the taking away of Elijah, to strengthen their faith.'

The one real crux is the phrase 'from thy head.' Ellicott canvasses the options with characteristic fullness: the word 'head' may signify 'self, or person, like the word soul' (comparing Genesis 40:13); or it may mean 'from over thy head, i.e., from his position of superiority over thee as thy master and teacher'; or, 'but very improbably,' it may be read literally 'away over thine head' of the ascension itself. John Wesley offers the same fork, that the phrase respects 'either the manner of sitting in schools, where the scholar sat at his master's feet,' or 'the manner of Elijah's translation, which was to be by a power sent from heaven.' John Gill, for his part, dwells on the striking location — Bethel, 'a place where one of Jeroboam's calves was set up,' yet also 'a school of the prophets, perhaps founded by Elijah as a nursery for religion, and a check upon the idolatry of the times.'

On Elisha's answer — 'Yea, I know it; hold ye your peace' — the witnesses are tender and agreed. Ellicott insists he says it 'not to prevent the gathering of a crowd, nor yet to intimate that his master's modesty will be shocked,' but 'simply to suggest that the subject is painful both to him and to his beloved master,' the Hebrew hehĕshû imitating the sound 'like our hush!' Wesley hears the same grief under discipline: 'Do not aggravate my grief, nor divert me with any unseasonable discourses.' Keil notes that Elisha knew Elijah's own feeling. The pastoral note on which they meet is Ellicott's and Wesley's together: a great loss is coming, and love best honours it not with talk but with a reverent, sorrowing silence.
4:
The verse repeats, almost exactly, the exchange of v.2 — 'Elisha, tarry here, I pray thee; for the LORD hath sent me to Jericho' — and the witnesses fasten on that very repetition. Charles Ellicott reads it as a mark of transmission: 'The exact repetition of the language of 2 Kings 2:2-3 in this and the next two verses appears to indicate that the narrative had originally been handed on by oral tradition, probably in the prophetic guilds at the local sanctuaries.' For the grammatical-historical school the monotony is not clumsiness but a fingerprint of the story's origin among the very schools Elijah is visiting.

Keil and Delitzsch take the repetition theologically rather than critically. Elijah repeated the appeal 'in Bethel, and again in Jericho, but always in vain,' and 'the taking away of Elijah had also been revealed to the disciples of the prophets at Jericho.' More importantly, they see design in the route itself: the course 'from Gilgal past Bethel and Jericho was not merely occasioned by the fact that he was obliged to touch at these places on the way to the Jordan, but had evidently also the same higher purpose' — the strengthening of the prophetic communities — 'for which his ascension to heaven had been revealed both to Elisha and to the disciples of the prophets.' The journey, in other words, is a farewell visitation, each stop a deliberate act of pastoral care.

John Gill simply anchors the geography — Elijah bids him tarry 'at Bethel' — and lets the scene move forward. The witnesses do not divide here; they cooperate. Where Ellicott hears the cadence of oral tradition preserved in the guilds, Keil hears the settled purpose of a prophet spending his last hours confirming the faithful. Both readings honour the same truth the chapter keeps pressing: that Elijah's going is not a solitary flight to glory but a ministry carried out to the end, gathering the sons of the prophets and, through them, holding up true religion 'in a time of general apostasy.' Elisha's unvarying reply — 'As the LORD liveth, and as thy soul liveth, I will not leave thee' — becomes, by its very sameness, the refrain of a devotion that will not be worn down by repetition, so that 'they two went on' together toward the river and the fire.
5:
The scene at Jericho mirrors Bethel almost line for line: the sons of the prophets come to Elisha with the same question, and receive the same answer, 'Yea, I know it; hold ye your peace.' The witnesses treat the two towns together, and John Gill pauses first on Jericho's paradox. Though the place had 'lately' been rebuilt 'under a curse to the builder' — the ancient sentence of Joshua fulfilled in Hiel — 'yet was blessed with a school of the prophets,' whose disciples 'came to Elisha, and said unto him' the very words spoken at Bethel, 'to whom he made the same reply.' That a nursery of piety should stand in a city built in defiance of a divine curse is, for Gill, a quiet emblem of grace planted where judgment had fallen.

Keil and Delitzsch, quoting the running narrative, gather the momentum of the passage: 'And Elijah said unto him, Tarry, I pray thee, here; for the LORD hath sent me to Jordan... And he said, As the LORD liveth... I will not leave thee. And they two went on.' The threefold appeal has now failed a third time, and the two press on to the river while 'fifty men of the sons of the prophets went, and stood to view afar off.' Keil sees the whole approach converging: master and disciple advancing alone, the guild following at a distance, the Jordan waiting to be divided. Charles Ellicott adds only the philologist's touch, noting that 'came' is more exactly 'drew near,' and that the disciples' 'answered' should read simply 'said' — small refinements that keep the reader close to the Hebrew.

Though the source material here is spare, the witnesses draw out its steady point. The repetition that Ellicott elsewhere traced to oral tradition now serves the reader as a rhythm of testing and constancy: at each stage the loss is announced, the sorrow acknowledged with a hushing word, and the resolve to follow renewed. Jericho, like Bethel, shows the schools of the prophets scattered through 'a time of general apostasy,' keeping true worship alive in the shadow of the calf and the curse. And Elisha, unmoved by every fresh warning, embodies the lesson the chapter presses on all who would receive a departing saint's blessing — not to shrink from the coming grief, but to cleave the closer as the parting nears, so that 'they two went on' to the brink of Jordan and the wonder about to unfold there.
6:
For the third and last time Elijah says 'tarry, I pray thee, here,' now at Jericho, 'for the LORD hath sent me to Jordan'; and for the third time Elisha binds himself with the oath and refuses. The source material at this verse is thin, but the witnesses draw the whole movement together. John Gill reads Elijah as 'seemingly very desirous to get rid of him, that he might not see his assumption' — the same reticence the commentators debated at v.2, whether from humility, tenderness, or a testing of Elisha's love. Whatever its root, the appeal fails a third time, and 'they two went on' alone toward the river.

Charles Ellicott, drawing on the French commentator Reuss, lifts the scene from repetition into pathos: 'Not only Elisha, the intimate companion and future successor of Elijah, but all the disciples of the different schools of the prophets have the presentiment of the loss which threatens them. The Spirit has warned them all; they communicate their fears, but Elisha forbids them to give free course to their sorrow. A respectful silence, a resignation not exempt from foreboding, suits this condition of things. Elisha clings to his master, as though he could keep him back; the disciples follow them with their eyes. The monotony of the successive scenes adds to the solemn effect of the total description.' It is a striking defence of the very repetition a hasty reader might call tedious: the sameness is the solemnity.

Ellicott also records the small textual currents around the verse — that the Syriac and Arabic add 'unto Elisha,' and that the Septuagint's 'and Elisha said' is 'an improvement' on the bare 'and he said.' But the weight of the witnesses falls on the human drama. Three towns, three dismissals, three refusals, and a company of foreboding disciples trailing behind: the chapter has been building a deliberate crescendo, and here it reaches the water's edge. What the commentators find in the scene is the dignity of a shared sorrow held in check — grief that does not clamour but keeps a 'respectful silence,' a disciple who will not let go, and a master walking steadily toward the place where God has appointed to take him. The reticence of Elijah and the reverence of the schools together frame the miracle that immediately follows, and prepare the reader, as they prepared Elisha, to watch to the very end.
7:
'And fifty men of the sons of the prophets went, and stood to view afar off: and they two stood by Jordan.' The witnesses ask a single, practical question about these fifty: what did they know, and why did they come? On knowledge, Adam Clarke is decisive — 'They fully expected this extraordinary event, and they could have known it only from Elijah himself, or by a direct revelation from God.' The whole prophetic community, that is, stood in conscious expectation of what was about to happen.

On motive, John Wesley guards the fifty against the charge of idle sightseeing. They came, he says, 'to observe this great event, Elijah's translation to heaven, which they expected every moment: and whereof they desired to be spectators, not to satisfy their own curiosity, but that they might be witnesses of it to others.' The distinction matters to Wesley: they are not gawkers but appointed witnesses, present so that the wonder should be established by testimony. John Gill reads their standing 'afar off' the same way — they came 'to have a view, if they could, of the assumption of Elijah to heaven, and be witnesses of it.'

Charles Ellicott supplies the topography. 'Stood to view' he renders 'taken their stand opposite, directly opposite the place where the two were standing by the brink of the river, yet at some distance behind'; and he catches their curiosity at a very human pitch: 'They wished to see whether and how the companions would cross the stream at a point where there was no ford.' The fifty, then, are watching a puzzle — two men at a fordless river — and are about to see it solved by a miracle. The witnesses converge on the providential design in their presence. Just as the disciples would be eye-witnesses of the ascension in Acts 1:9, so these fifty are stationed by God to see Elijah taken, that the church might not lack testimony to so singular a departure. Matthew Henry, at the previous verse, had put it memorably: God so ordered it 'that they might be eye-witnesses of the honour heaven did to that prophet who was despised and rejected of men.' The company standing 'afar off' is thus no accident of the narrative but the appointed jury of a heavenly act.
8:
Elijah rolls up his mantle, smites the Jordan, and the waters part 'hither and thither, so that they two went over on dry ground.' The witnesses read the act on three levels — the plain miracle, the symbolic garment, and the typological water. On the miracle itself Adam Clarke is blunt: 'This was a most astonishing miracle, and could be performed only by the almighty power of God.' Charles Ellicott sets it in a series — 'a symbolical action like that of Moses smiting the rock, or stretching out his rod over the sea' — and draws the sacramental principle the school prizes: 'In all these cases the outward and visible sign is made the channel of the invisible and spiritual force of faith,' the parting recalling Exodus 14:21 and the crossing of Joshua 4:22.

On the mantle the commentators turn antiquarian. John Gill weighs whether it was the hairy garment or 'a shorter robe,' and cites the Septuagint's melotes, 'a goat's skin, hanging down from the neck.' Clarke agrees it was a sheepskin, and expands on the custom: 'The skins of beasts, dressed with the hair on, were formerly worn by prophets and priests as the simple insignia of their office,' a badge that survives, he notes, in academic hoods and royal ermine. Ellicott fixes it as the hairy addereth that 'characterised him as prophet,' comparing Zechariah 13:4. The garment is thus no ordinary cloak but the emblem of the prophetic office, which is why its transfer to Elisha will carry such weight.

It is John Wesley who lifts the verse to its pastoral height, in words that echo Matthew Henry's on v.1: 'These waters of old yielded to the ark, now to the prophet's mantle, which to those that wanted the ark was an equivalent token of God's presence. When God will take his children to himself, death is the Jordan, which they must pass through. And they find a way through it, a safe and comfortable way. The death of Christ has divided those waters, that the ransomed of the Lord may pass over.' The witnesses, whatever their differing angles, meet on this figure: the divided river is a picture of the believer's death made passable by Christ, so that the same triumph Henry voiced — 'O death, where is thy sting?' — sounds again over the dry ground where Elijah and Elisha crossed.
9:
Across the river Elijah offers his heir one request — 'Ask what I shall do for thee, before I be taken away' — and Elisha asks 'a double portion of thy spirit.' The witnesses debate hard over what exactly he asked, and unanimously reject the crudest reading. Keil and Delitzsch catalogue the errors: the request 'has been misunderstood by many, who have supposed that Elisha wished to have a double measure of Elijah's spirit,' as though he sought twice what his master possessed. Adam Clarke agrees the words 'mean rather two parts than double the quantity,' and grounds the phrase in the law of Deuteronomy 21:17, where the firstborn receives 'a Double Portion of all that he hath.' Elisha, Clarke says, 'considered himself the only child or first-born of Elijah.'

Charles Ellicott makes the point exactly: the request is not 'Give me twice as great a share of the spirit of prophecy as thou possessest thyself,' but 'Let me be the firstborn among thy spiritual sons; make me thy true spiritual heir' — twice as great a share of Elijah's spirit 'as any of the rest' of the sons of the prophets. John Wesley reads it the same way, 'double to what the rest of the sons of the prophets receive,' and then notes the sequel with wonder: 'though Elisha desired no more, yet God gave him more than he desired or expected; and he seems to have had a greater portion of the gifts of God's Spirit than even Elijah had.'

Matthew Henry, whose exposition anchors the verse, draws out the piety of the asking. Elisha 'asks not for wealth, nor honour, nor exemption from trouble, but to be qualified for the service of God and his generation.' And he asks rightly — not 'Give me the Spirit,' for 'he knew very well it was God's gift,' but 'Let it be upon me, intercede with God for this for me.' Henry hears in it a holy ambition: 'It is a holy ambition to covet earnestly the best gifts, and those which will render us most serviceable to God and our brethren.' John Gill only underscores the rarity — 'not a common privilege, but what is rarely enjoyed, and difficult to obtain.' The witnesses close in agreement: Elisha's prayer is the model petition of a successor, seeking not to outshine his father but to inherit his father's office and Spirit for the work still to be done.
10:
'Thou hast asked a hard thing: nevertheless, if thou see me when I am taken from thee, it shall be so unto thee; but if not, it shall not be so.' The witnesses agree first on why the thing is 'hard.' It is not, they insist, too hard for God, but beyond Elijah's power to grant. John Gill calls it 'not a common privilege, but what is rarely enjoyed'; Adam Clarke says plainly, 'This is what is not in my power, God alone can give this'; and Charles Ellicott makes it the hinge of the verse: 'to grant such a petition was not in Elijah's own power, but in God's only,' so that the prophet 'connects the fulfilment of his follower's wish with a condition depending entirely upon the Divine will.' Elijah thus 'disclaims power to fulfil the request.'

The harder question is the meaning of the sign — 'if thou see me when I am taken.' Here the commentators diverge in emphasis. Adam Clarke resists any magical reading: 'on the mere seeing or not seeing him in the moment in which he was taken away, this Divine gift could not depend'; he takes it to mean simply, 'If thou continue with me till I am translated, God will grant this to thee.' The seeing, for Clarke, is shorthand for faithful perseverance to the end. John Wesley reads the sign more instrumentally: it was proposed 'not without the direction of God's Spirit, that hereby he might engage him more earnestly to wait, and more fervently to pray for this mercy.' Ellicott, following Keil and Thenius, holds the condition together with the mystery — 'If the Lord think thee worthy to witness my departure, thou wilt be worthy to win thy boon' — and adds that the wording implies Elijah's going will be 'something exalted above the perception of ordinary men.'

Matthew Henry, expounding the verse under v.9, gathers the pastoral fruit. Elijah's calling it 'a hard thing' teaches Elisha to 'put a due value upon it,' for 'those are best prepared for spiritual blessings that are most sensible of their worth and their own unworthiness to receive them.' And the condition of keeping close to his master 'even to the last' shows that 'a diligent attendance upon his master's instructions, and a careful observance of his example, particularly now in his last scene,' are the appointed means of obtaining much of his spirit. The witnesses land together on a quiet doctrine of grace: God gives his best gifts to those who prize them, persevere for them, and pray for them, so that the sign of seeing Elijah taken is really the seal on a faith that watched to the end — as the disciples would watch their Lord ascend in Acts 1:10.
11:
'There appeared a chariot of fire, and horses of fire... and Elijah went up by a whirlwind into heaven.' On the plainest sense the witnesses are agreed: Elijah was truly translated, not merely died. Adam Clarke defends it against rationalizing — 'He was truly translated; and the words here leave us no room' for the conjecture that he merely passed to another planet — for 'we are told that Elijah went up into heaven.' John Wesley, following Matthew Henry, sees in the fiery convoy the ministry of angels: 'In this form the angels appeared,' sent visibly because 'Elijah being to carry his body with him, this heavenly guard appeared visibly... in the form of a chariot and horses, that he may ride in state, may ride in triumph, like a prince, like a conqueror.'

But the verse also provokes the chapter's sharpest interpretive division, and Charles Ellicott presses it hard. The Hebrew, he argues, must not be taken 'in too literal a sense.' Sĕ'ârâh is the storm-blast, and 'the Hebrew mind recognised the presence and working of Jehovah in the terrific phenomena of nature; the thunder-cloud was His chariot, the thunder His voice, the lightning His arrow.' Hence 'the popular conception, which we see embodied in such pictures as William Blake's Translation of Elijah, that the prophet ascended in a fiery car drawn by horses of fire, is plainly read into, rather than gathered from, the sacred text.' The essential meaning, for Ellicott, 'is this, that God suddenly took Elijah to Himself, amid a grand display of His power in and through the forces of nature' — and he even entertains Bähr's suggestion that 'went up' may mean 'disappeared, was consumed,' comparing Psalms 107:26.

The others hold to the fiery reality more firmly. Keil and Delitzsch, cited alongside, describe 'a fiery phenomenon, which appeared to the eyes of Elisha as a chariot of fire with horses of fire, in which Elijah rode,' aligning it with the horses and chariots of fire round Elisha in 2 Kings 6:17. John Gill keeps the reader's eye on the talk that filled the last moments — of the Spirit's gifts, the state of religion, the training of prophets, Elisha's succession. The witnesses, however they weigh the imagery, converge on the same double marvel: that God honoured his zealous servant with a departure of unearthly glory, and that, as Wesley closes, 'he who had burned with holy zeal for God and his honour was now conveyed in fire into his immediate presence' — a foreshadowing, Henry adds, of Christ's own ascension.
12:
Elisha sees his master borne away and cries, 'My father, my father, the chariot of Israel, and the horsemen thereof!' — then rends his clothes in two. The witnesses read the cry as a double tribute. On 'My father,' Keil and Delitzsch explain that Elisha called Elijah so 'as his spiritual father, who had begotten him as his son through the word of God,' and John Wesley that it expressed both 'his fatherly affection' and 'his fatherly authority,' since 'the scholars of the prophets are called their sons.' Matthew Henry catches the desolation in it: 'He saw his own condition like that of a fatherless child thrown upon the world, and lamented it accordingly.'

On the second title — 'the chariot of Israel, and the horsemen thereof' — the commentators are strikingly of one mind, and lean on the ancient Aramaic paraphrase. Adam Clarke gives the Chaldee: 'My master, my master! who, by thy intercession, wast of more use to Israel than horses and chariots.' Charles Ellicott concurs — the phrase expresses 'what Elijah was to the nation,' for 'the personal work and influence of a prophet like Elijah was the truest safeguard of Israel,' more than the chariots and horsemen that 'constituted, in that age, the chief military arm.' Keil reads it identically, 'a symbolical representation of the strong defence which Elijah had been through his ministry to the kingdom of Israel,' comparing the same words spoken over Elisha himself in 2 Kings 13:14. Wesley sums it: Elijah 'by thy example, and counsels, and prayers, and power with God, didst more for the defence and preservation of Israel than all their chariots and horses.'

The rending of the garments 'in two pieces' the witnesses take for a sign of extreme grief — Keil, 'from the top to the bottom, as a proof of the greatness of his sorrow.' John Gill alone turns the eye upward, noting how closely 'the ascension of Elijah' answers to 'the ascension of Christ,' so that Elisha, having seen it, 'had the token by which he might expect to have the double portion, as the disciples after the ascension of Christ had an extraordinary effusion of the Spirit.' Here the two threads of the chapter meet: the seeing that fulfils the condition of v.10, and the grief of a nation losing its true defence. The witnesses land on a sober truth — that a faithful prophet is worth more to a people than its armies, and that his loss, though he goes to glory, is rightly mourned with rent garments and a fatherless cry.
13:
Elisha 'took up also the mantle of Elijah that fell from him,' and the witnesses read the fallen cloak as the great token of succession. Keil and Delitzsch put it exactly: 'The prophet's mantle of the master fell to Elisha the disciple, as a pledge to himself that his request was fulfilled, and as a visible sign to others that he was his divinely appointed successor, and that the spirit of Elijah rested upon him.' Adam Clarke agrees that 'his having the mantle was a proof that he was invested with the authority and influence of his master,' the same cloak with which Elijah had divided Jordan and by which Elisha had first been called in 1 Kings 19:19.

Matthew Henry, whose exposition fills the verse, draws out both the tenderness and the theology of the moment. Elijah 'did not let fall his body as others do' at death; instead 'he let fall his mantle instead of it, for he was unclothed, that he might be clothed upon with immortality' — going 'to a world where he needed not the mantle to adorn him, nor to shelter him from the weather.' Elisha took it up 'not as a sacred relic to be worshipped, but as a significant garment to be worn' and a replacement for his own rent clothes; and Henry widens the lesson: 'There are remains of great and good men, which, like this mantle, ought to be gathered up and preserved by the survivors — their sayings, their writings, their examples — that, as their works follow them in the reward, they may stay behind in the benefit.' John Wesley echoes the point: the mantle fell 'as a pledge that together with Elijah's mantle his Spirit should rest upon him.'

John Gill and Charles Ellicott confirm the plain sense — the mantle 'the badge of the prophet's office was naturally transferred to his successor.' But Henry presses the caution that guards the whole scene from superstition. The garment matters not as a charm but as a token; its worth lies wholly in 'the descent of the Spirit' it signified, so that it was 'more than if he had bequeathed to him thousands of gold and silver.' The witnesses meet on a searching application, which Henry will sharpen at the next verse: it profits nothing to inherit the mantles, the places, or the books of the saints who have gone before if we have not their spirit and their God. Elisha returns to Jordan clothed in his father's cloak, but the real inheritance is invisible — the Spirit that made the cloak mighty.
14:
Back at the river Elisha smites the water with Elijah's mantle and cries, 'Where is the LORD God of Elijah?' — and the waters part for him as they had for his master. The witnesses read the question as faith, not doubt. Charles Ellicott calls it 'a sort of irony of faith': 'Has He left the earth with His prophet? If not, let Him now show His power, and verify the granting of my request.' Elisha 'seeks Jehovah as the only source of power,' Ellicott adds, comparing the reproach of Jeremiah 2:6 against those who fail to ask, 'Where is Jehovah?' John Gill reads the act as a deliberate test 'to make trial whether he had the same spirit and power conferred on him.'

Matthew Henry, expounding the verse, refuses every lower construction of the cry. Elisha 'does not ask, Where is Elijah? as poring upon the loss of him... nor as expecting help from him; no, Elijah is happy, but is neither omniscient nor omnipotent; but he asks, Where is the Lord God of Elijah?' The prophet inquires 'after God' when 'our creature-comforts are removed' — and after 'the God that Elijah served, and honoured, and pleaded for, and adhered to when all Israel had deserted him.' Henry's threefold moral follows: the saints on earth should inquire after the God of the fathers; he is near to all who call; and 'those that walk in the spirit and steps of their godly faithful predecessors shall certainly experience the same grace,' for 'Elijah's God will be Elisha's too.'

The one genuine crux is textual — the puzzling aph hu, 'even he.' Keil and Delitzsch attach it to the question: 'Where is Jehovah the God of Elijah, yea He?' Charles Ellicott reports the whole tangle honestly, that 'there is clearly something wrong,' that the Septuagint merely transliterated the word, and that scholars variously emend it to 'then' or 'where.' Adam Clarke records the strange Vulgate reading in which Elisha smote the waters once in vain — 'and they were not divided' — and only after invoking the God of Elijah struck again successfully, a distinction Clarke notes 'is not followed by any of the other versions.' The Jamieson-Fausset-Brown fragment attached here is plainly misplaced and has nothing to do with the verse. Through the textual thicket the witnesses hold one clear point, best voiced by Henry: 'The Lord God of the holy prophets is the same yesterday, to-day, and for ever; and what will it avail us to have the mantles of those that are gone... if we have not their spirit, their God?'
15:
The sons of the prophets watching from Jericho see Elisha divide the Jordan and declare, 'The spirit of Elijah doth rest on Elisha,' then bow to the ground before him. The witnesses agree that the repeated miracle was the proof. Adam Clarke calls their conclusion 'a natural conclusion, from seeing him with the mantle, and working the same miracle,' which 'disposed them to yield the same obedience to him they had done to his master.' Charles Ellicott renders 'doth rest' more exactly as 'hath alighted, settled, rested,' and confirms 'the proof was that Elisha had just repeated his master's miracle.' Keil and Delitzsch read the same recognition into the bowing.

A small question divides the commentators: who exactly are these watchers? Ellicott weighs whether they are 'the fifty who went and stood opposite afar off' at v.7 or a wider group, and concludes 'on the whole it seems likely that all the guild residing at Jericho is meant,' assembled at the riverside to witness the miracle 'which was evidence to them that Elisha was to be their future head.' John Gill pictures them as those 'who went out from thence towards Jordan, to have a sight if they could of the assumption of Elijah,' and who now saw Elisha cross the parted waters.

It is Matthew Henry and John Wesley who draw the pastoral edge, and they draw it identically — a lesson about honouring God's evident presence over human pedigree. The sons of the prophets 'were trained up in the schools; Elisha was taken from the plough,' yet 'when they perceived that God was with him, and that this was the man whom he delighted to honour, they readily submitted to him as their head and father, as the people to Joshua when Moses was dead,' recalling the willing obedience of Joshua 1:17. Wesley presses the application in a sentence the witnesses would all endorse: 'Those that appear to have God's Spirit and presence with them ought to have our esteem and best affections, notwithstanding the meanness of their extraction and education.' Henry adds that this ready submission 'was a great encouragement to Elisha, and helped to clear his call.' The scene closes the story of succession begun with the fallen mantle: the office has passed, the Spirit is seen to rest, and the community bows — not to a man of rank, but to the man on whom God's hand visibly lies.
16:
The sons of the prophets, unable to grasp that Elijah is simply gone, beg leave to send 'fifty strong men' to search the mountains and valleys, 'lest peradventure the Spirit of the LORD hath taken him up, and cast him upon some mountain, or into some valley.' The witnesses divide gently over the searchers' motive. John Wesley offers two possibilities: either 'that God had not finally taken him away from them, but only for a time,' or 'that God had only taken away his soul, and that his body was cast down into some place, which they desired to seek, that they might give it an honourable burial.' Adam Clarke reads their reasoning charitably too — though they had seen him 'taken up towards heaven, yet they thought it possible that the Spirit of the Lord might have descended with him, and left him on some remote mountain or valley.'

Charles Ellicott corrects Keil on the aim of the search: the disciples' motive 'was not a desire to pay the last honours to the body of the departed master... for they rather expected to find Elijah alive.' He identifies the fifty as perhaps 'attendants on the members of the prophetic guild,' their being 'sons of valour' important 'as the search in the mountains would involve danger.' And he draws a striking exegetical inference from their very confusion: their suggestion 'is a good comment on 2 Kings 2:11-12. It shows that what is there told is certainly not that Elijah ascended a fiery chariot and rode visibly into heaven, as the popular notion is' — for men who had watched him go would not then propose to hunt for him among the hills. He compares the Spirit's carrying away of 1 Kings 18:12 and of Philip in Acts 8:39.

Elisha's flat refusal — 'Ye shall not send' — the witnesses read as settled certainty. Adam Clarke: 'He knew that he was translated to heaven, and that therefore it would be useless.' John Gill notes only that the fifty were young, 'stout, and strong, and able to travel for days together.' The contrast the commentators leave standing is instructive: Elisha, who had seen and understood, needs no search; the disciples, warned yet unbelieving, cannot rest until they have looked. Ellicott's point becomes the verse's quiet theological gain — the sober confusion of eyewitnesses guards the text against a crudely literal picture, while Elisha's calm 'Ye shall not send' shows the difference between those who have truly received the sign and those who still grope after a body God has taken to himself.
17:
Elisha yields at last — 'when they urged him till he was ashamed, he said, Send' — and the fifty search three days without finding Elijah. The witnesses fasten on that word 'ashamed,' and read Elisha's consent not as weakness but as gracious forbearance. John Gill takes it plainly: he was pressed 'to deny them any longer, being so very pressing and importunate.' Adam Clarke agrees: 'He saw they would not be satisfied unless they made the proposed search; he felt therefore that he could not, with any good grace, resist their importunity any longer.' John Wesley supplies the delicate reason — Elisha yielded 'lest they should think his denial proceeded from a neglect of his master, or a contempt of them.'

Charles Ellicott presses the Hebrew, 'unto being ashamed,' and canvasses the shades of meaning: 'They pressed upon him until he was embarrassed, disconcerted, put out of countenance.' He records the range of scholarly renderings — Thenius preferring 'they carried their importunity to a shameless length,' Keil and Bähr 'until he was disappointed in the hope of dissuading them' — and compares the same idiom in 2 Kings 8:11 and Judges 3:25. Keil and Delitzsch, quoting forward, simply carry the narrative on: 'They sought three days, but found him not.'

It is Matthew Henry, expounding under v.16, who mines the episode for wisdom. Elisha 'consented not to their motion till they overcame him with importunity,' and Henry draws the maxim: 'Wise men may yield to that, for the sake of peace and the good opinion of others, which yet their judgment is against as needless and fruitless.' The three fruitless days, moreover, would serve a purpose: 'This would make them the more willing to acquiesce in his judgment another time.' The witnesses converge on a gently practical lesson about the limits of insistence. Elisha knew the search was vain, yet he let the sons of the prophets learn it for themselves rather than force his certainty upon them; and their empty-handed return would settle, better than any argument, both his authority and the fact of Elijah's translation. The verse thus quietly commends a pastor's patience — bearing with the honest doubts of the grieving, even to the point of letting them prove those doubts wrong by experience.
18:
The searchers return empty-handed to Elisha, who had stayed at Jericho, and he meets them with a mild reproach: 'Did I not say unto you, Go not?' The source here is spare, but the witnesses draw out its close. John Gill simply notes that Elisha 'tarried at Jericho, waiting their return to hear the report they made.' Charles Ellicott sharpens the Hebrew: 'Now he (emphatic) was abiding in Jericho' while they searched, and 'Did I not say' may be rendered 'Did I not command,' for 'Elisha could now fairly remind them of his authority' — the phrase 'Go not' being, in the Hebrew, imperative, just as 'Ye shall not send' had been at v.16. The new prophet's word, doubted three days before, is now vindicated, and he can press it as command.

Ellicott then marks the larger significance: 'With these words, the history of Elijah significantly closes.' He quotes Reuss to fine effect: 'Elias resembled Moses in courage and eloquence, and no other prophet was his equal. But when he withdrew from the world, that Providence which guided the destinies of Israel did not therefore forsake His people. A portion of Elijah's spirit passed to his disciples; and they are forbidden to seek their departed master in the desert: they must find among themselves the means of carrying on his work.' The refusal to search, on this reading, is more than a rebuke of curiosity; it is a theological full stop. Elijah is not to be recovered but succeeded.

Ellicott also relays the source-critical view of Thenius, who 'considers the entire section from 2 Kings 1:2 to 2 Kings 2:18 to be a distinct fragment of a lost history of Elijah,' its contents betraying 'the same spirit as 1 Kings 17-19' — a judgment he reports without endorsing. Matthew Henry, treating the verse under v.16, gives the pastoral summary the witnesses rest on: the messengers 'returned with a non est inventus, he is not to be found,' and gave Elisha occasion to remind them of their folly. 'Traversing hills and valleys will never bring us to Elijah,' Henry concludes, 'but the imitation of his holy faith and zeal will, in due time.' The chapter's first half thus ends where it must — not at a grave, but at a succession; the way to the translated saints is not a search party but a holy life.
19:
The men of Jericho lay their trouble before the new prophet: 'the situation of this city is pleasant... but the water is naught, and the ground barren.' The witnesses begin with the paradox of the place. Matthew Henry marvels that Jericho, 'built in disobedience to a command, in defiance to a threatening, and at the expense of the lives of all the builder's children,' should yet become 'a nursery of piety,' so that 'even within those walls that were built by iniquity we find' the sons of the prophets. 'Fools,' he says wryly, 'build houses for wise men to dwell in,' and 'here the wealth of the sinner provided a habitation for the just' — the very city where Christ would one day lodge with Zacchaeus in Luke 19:1.

On the nature of the trouble the grammatical-historical voices refine the English. Charles Ellicott observes that the town, 'the city of palms,' had 'a fine position, rising like an oasis from a broad plain of sand'; the fault lay not in the soil but the spring. 'The water is naught' means simply 'bad,' and the clause our versions render 'the ground barren' is better taken of its effect on living things — 'the country bears dead births' or 'has many miscarriages,' for v.21 shows 'the waters, not the soil, were the cause of the evil complained of.' Adam Clarke puts it compactly: 'The barrenness of the ground was the effect of the badness of the water.' John Wesley weighs whether the curse was original 'or became so from the curse of God inflicted upon it when Hiel rebuilt it,' and notes that after Elisha's cure the region 'grew exceeding fruitful.'

Matthew Henry draws the pastoral point that unites the exposition. 'God's faithful prophets love to be employed; it is wisdom to make use of them during the little while that their light is with us.' The men of Jericho had not troubled Elijah, 'perhaps because he was not so easy of access,' but now, softened 'by the influence of the divinity-school in their city,' they come to Elisha. And Henry teaches gratitude for common mercies: 'Water is a common mercy, which we should estimate by the greatness of the calamity which the want or unwholesomeness of it would be.' The witnesses set the scene for the miracle to follow by insisting that a prophet's business is to make every place 'some way or other the better for them' — to sweeten bitter waters and barren ground, as a figure of what grace does for bitter and barren souls.
20:
Elisha's remedy is deliberately strange: 'Bring me a new cruse, and put salt therein.' The witnesses fasten on both the newness of the vessel and the unfitness of the salt, and read each as a lesson that the cure is God's, not nature's. On the new cruse, John Gill explains it was 'one that had never been used, that it might not be thought that the virtue was owing to anything that had been, or was, put into it.' Charles Ellicott agrees the vessel was new 'because the holy purpose demanded an instrument uncontaminated by use,' comparing the unused cart and the red heifer of Numbers 19:2. John Wesley reads it sacramentally, 'that there might be no legal pollution in it which might offend God, and hinder his miraculous operation.'

The salt provokes the sharper observation, for it is exactly the wrong medicine. John Wesley makes this the point: salt is 'a most improper remedy, for salt naturally makes waters brackish, and lands barren. Hereby therefore he would shew that this was effected solely by the Divine power, which could work either without means, or against them.' Charles Ellicott reads the salt more positively as a fitting sign — 'an antiseptic, an appropriate sacramental medium of the Divine influence which was to expel the corruption of the spring.' The two are not far apart: whether one stresses the salt's natural contrariety or its symbolic aptness, both agree the healing power lay wholly outside the element used.

The pastoral weight of the verse, drawn out by Matthew Henry at v.19, lies in the fact that the sufferers must fetch the cruse and the salt themselves. 'If salt had been proper to season the water, yet what could so small a quantity do towards it? But thus those that would be helped must be employed and have their faith and obedience tried. God's works of grace are wrought, not by any operations of ours, but in observance of his institutions.' The witnesses meet on a single doctrine: God heals by means He appoints, means that of themselves could never accomplish the work, so that the glory returns entirely to Him. The salt is emptied of any natural claim; the cruse is emptied of any prior use; and Jericho's fountain is healed not by a chemistry but by a word, a picture — as Henry will say — of how grace seasons and cleanses what nature had left bitter and dead.
21:
Elisha goes 'unto the spring of the waters' and casts in the salt with the word, 'Thus saith the LORD, I have healed these waters.' The witnesses stress first that he struck at the source, not the stream. Adam Clarke notes 'he cast in the salt at the place where the waters sprang out of the earth,' and draws the miracle's permanence from it: 'If the salt could have had any natural tendency to render the water salubrious, it could have acted only for a short time, and only on that portion which now arose from the spring... But the miracle here was permanent,' the waters becoming 'permanently fit for all agricultural and domestic uses.' He cites the Jewish commentator Jarchi, who reasoned that since 'salt is a thing which corrupts water, it is evident that this was a true miracle.'

Charles Ellicott presses the same theology onto the fountain — 'Aines Sultân, the Sultan's Fountain, a fine spring of sweet water which irrigates the neighbouring plain' — insisting it was 'not the prophet's own power, nor the natural virtues of the salt, but the Divine creative will' that healed it. He carefully re-renders the effect: the waters would no longer cause 'death' to people or 'their unborn offspring,' and the clause about barren land is better 'making abortion,' picking up the exact word of v.19. John Wesley glosses 'death' as 'hurt, or danger, to man or beast, by drinking of it.'

The word of authority draws the witnesses' warmest comment. Matthew Henry, at v.19, observes that Elisha 'did not pretend to do this by his own power, but in God's name: Thus saith the Lord, I have healed these waters. He is but the instrument, the channel through which God is pleased to convey this healing virtue.' And Henry finds a strategy of grace in it: by helping Jericho 'with a Thus saith the Lord, they would be made the more willing hereafter to receive from him a reproof, admonition, or command, with the same preface.' The witnesses close on the spiritual figure the miracle invites — that 'the way to reform men's lives is to renew their hearts; let those be seasoned with the salt of grace, for out of them are the issues of life.' Heal the spring and you heal every stream; renew the heart and you cleanse the whole course of a life. The barren, death-dealing fountain of Jericho becomes, under a prophet's word, a standing emblem of what God's grace does at the source.
22:
'So the waters were healed unto this day, according to the saying of Elisha.' The verse rests on a single witness, John Gill, but he draws it out at length, tracing the miracle's endurance far beyond the sacred text. The healing held 'not only unto the time of the writer of this history, but to the times of Josephus, as he testifies, and even to ours; for there is a spring now called Elisha's spring or fountain.' Gill quotes the traveller Maundrell, who found 'its waters received in a basin about nine or ten paces long,' issuing 'in good plenty' and 'dispersing their refreshment to all the field between this and Jericho, and rendering it exceeding fruitful.' Other travellers reported 'the water is very clear and cool, and issues in a copious stream,' and Pliny had long before given it the name 'Calirroe, the beautiful stream,' speaking of it as 'hot, wholesome, and medicinal, and of great note.'

The force of Gill's collected testimony is theological, and it turns on the phrase 'unto this day.' Charles Ellicott, at v.21, had already fixed its sense as 'the time when the narrative was first committed to writing' — but Gill and later observers press it further, that the fountain still flows sweet, so that the endurance itself becomes a standing confirmation. What God does by His word is not a passing marvel that fades when the prophet departs, but a lasting change in the nature of the thing. The bad water did not merely improve for a season; it was 'healed,' its property altered.

Matthew Henry, expounding the miracle at v.19, had drawn exactly this permanence into a promise of grace, citing Ecclesiastes 3:14: 'What God does shall be for ever. When he, by his Spirit, heals a soul, there shall be no more death nor barrenness; the property is altered — what was useless and offensive becomes grateful and serviceable.' The witnesses, though few at this verse, land on a settled comfort. The lasting sweetness of Elisha's fountain, still testified to by travellers and geographers centuries on, is a parable of the durability of God's saving work: a heart truly healed by grace does not relapse into its old bitterness, for the cure reaches the spring and changes the nature. 'According to the saying of Elisha which he spake in the name of the Lord' — and that word, once spoken, stood for ever.
23:
Going up to Bethel, Elisha is met by 'little children' who jeer, 'Go up, thou bald head; go up, thou bald head' — and the witnesses labour above all to establish who these mockers were and how grave their offence. The great question is the Hebrew nearim ketannim. Adam Clarke mounts the fullest case that these were no toddlers: naar 'signifies not only a child, but a young man, a servant, or even a soldier,' noting that Isaac was called naar at twenty-eight in Genesis 21:5 and Joseph at thirty-nine in Genesis 41:12. John Wesley agrees the word 'often signifies... those who are grown up to maturity,' and reckons 'they were old enough to discern between good and evil.' Charles Ellicott moderates: they were 'young boys' of 'various ages, between six or seven years and twenty,' since 'little children would not be likely to hit upon a biting sarcasm, nor to sally forth in a body.'

On the taunt itself the commentators find blasphemy, not mere rudeness. Adam Clarke hears in 'Go up' the gross insult, 'Ascend, thou empty skull, to heaven, as it is pretended thy master did! This was blasphemy against God.' John Wesley reads it the same way — 'Go up into heaven, whither thou pretendest Elijah is gone' — a scoff 'proceeding from a rooted impiety and hatred of God and his prophets.' Ellicott, more cautiously, doubts the Bethelites knew of the ascension at all, and takes 'go up' of the road Elisha was climbing, his prophet's mantle attracting notice; the baldness, he adds, 'was a reproach' and even 'suspicious as one of the marks of leprosy,' Elisha perhaps 'bald prematurely.'

Matthew Henry, whose exposition runs through the episode, sets the mockery in its national context. To reproach a man 'for his natural infirmities' is wicked, 'adding affliction to the afflicted'; yet the real target 'was his character as a prophet that they designed to abuse.' These children 'said as they were taught; they had learned of their idolatrous parents to call foul names,' and 'these young cocks crowed after the old ones,' Bethel being the seat of Jeroboam's calf. John Gill notes Elisha came to Bethel 'to acquaint the sons of the prophets with the assumption of Elijah' and 'confirm his own authority.' The witnesses converge on the point that will govern the terrible sequel: this was no childish prank but 'one of the crying sins of Israel,' the mocking of God's messengers foretold in 2 Chronicles 36:16 — impiety learned at home and aimed, through the prophet, at God Himself.
24:
Elisha turns, 'looked on them, and cursed them in the name of the LORD,' and two she-bears tear 'forty and two children.' No verse in the chapter presses the witnesses harder, for they must defend both the prophet and God against the charge of cruelty. Their first move is to lift the act out of personal revenge. John Gill reads the turning and looking as a warning meant 'to intimidate them, and make them ashamed... but to no purpose.' Charles Ellicott says the curse was uttered 'to avenge the honour of Jehovah, violated in his person,' and Keil and Delitzsch insist the supposed 'immorality of cursing' would fall not on Elisha but 'back upon the Lord God, who executed the curse of His servant.'

The commentators then answer the objection point by point. John Wesley mounts the fullest defence: the punishment was not too great 'if it be considered that their mocking proceeded from a great malignity of mind against God'; that they scorned 'not only a man, and an ancient man... and a prophet, but even God himself, and that glorious work of God, the assumption of Elijah'; that Bethel's idolatry 'by God's law deserved death'; and that Elisha acted 'not from any revengeful passion, but by the motion of God's Spirit, and by God's command.' Even the children's death, Wesley urges, if any were innocent, took them from 'that education which was most likely to expose them to eternal destruction.' Keil and Ellicott add that the mobbing may have been 'premeditated,' that forty-two 'may be a definite for an indefinite number' showing the crowd 'was considerable,' and that the event 'belongs to that dispensation in which judgment was made more prominent than mercy,' fulfilling the menace of Leviticus 26:21.

Matthew Henry, expounding at v.23, holds the two poles the witnesses balance. 'The prophet must be justified, for he did it by divine impulse. Had the curse come from any bad principle God would not have said Amen to it.' Elisha 'knew by the Spirit the bad character of these children' and 'what mischievous enemies they would be to God's prophets if they should live to be men.' And 'God must be glorified as a righteous God, that hates sin, and will reckon for it, even in little children.' Henry turns it, finally, to fear and to nurture: 'Let little children be afraid of speaking wicked words, for God notices what they say,' and let parents 'train them up well... for in vain do we look for good from those children whose education we have neglected.' The witnesses do not soften the severity; they locate it in God's own justice, provoked at Bethel not by baldness but by a settled, taught contempt for Him and His word.
25:
The chapter closes quietly: 'And he went from thence to mount Carmel, and from thence he returned to Samaria.' The witnesses read the itinerary as a deliberate rhythm of solitude and service. On Carmel, Keil and Delitzsch judge that Elisha went there 'probably to strengthen himself in solitude for the continuation of his master's work,' the mountain where Elijah himself had wrought his great contest with Baal in 1 Kings 18:19. Charles Ellicott agrees he retired 'to cultivate the memory of his master in solitude,' since Carmel's 'caves were well fitted for solitude and concealment,' and 'Elisha may have retired thither to prepare himself for his public ministry by prayer and fasting,' comparing our Lord's withdrawal in Matthew 4:1. John Gill notes only that Carmel was a place Elijah 'used to frequent, and where also there might be a school of the prophets.'

The return to Samaria the commentators read as the counter-movement to Carmel's retreat. Keil observes that Elisha went back to Samaria, 'where, according to 2 Kings 6:32, he possessed a house' — his settled and 'permanent abode,' as Ellicott adds. Carmel is for prayer; Samaria, the royal capital, is for work. Matthew Henry, closing the chapter, catches the balance in a maxim from Bishop Hall: to Carmel Elisha withdrew for retirement and contemplation, then 'returned to Samaria, where, being a public place, this father of the prophets might be most serviceable' — for 'he can never be a profitable seer who is either always or never alone.'

The witnesses land the chapter, then, on a portrait of the well-ordered prophetic life. Elisha has divided the Jordan, healed a fountain, and become the instrument of a fearful judgment; now he neither rushes on to the next act nor hides permanently from the world, but alternates the mountain and the city, the closet and the court. The solitude of Carmel replenishes what the service of Samaria will spend. Where the first half of the chapter had shown a master taken up and a disciple confirmed, the last verse shows that disciple settling into the pattern that will mark his fifty-year ministry — drawing strength from God in secret, then bringing it back among the people. Bishop Hall's balance, endorsed by all the witnesses, becomes the closing counsel: a servant of God must be neither always alone, and so useless, nor never alone, and so empty.
